6 min read
1 hour ago

--

In my previous article, I explained how to build a Configurable UI in React — an architecture where the backend controls the UI through configuration, and the frontend acts as a rendering engine. Instead of hardcoding forms, dashboards, or workflows, React dynamically builds the interface based on the configuration it receives.
That approach makes applications incredibly flexible. You can introduce new screens, update layouts, enable features, or modify forms without redeploying your frontend.
However, there’s another side to this architecture that deserves just as much attention: security.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Secure Configurable UI — theplainscriptWhen your frontend starts rendering UI based on external configuration, you’re no longer just building components — you’re building a system that trusts data coming from outside your application. If that trust isn’t managed carefully, a misconfigured API, an unexpected payload, or even a malicious configuration can expose sensitive functionality or introduce security vulnerabilities.
This article is a continuation of the previous one. Instead of focusing on how to build a Configurable UI, we’ll focus on how to secure it.
We’ll cover practical security techniques such as:

• Validating configuration before rendering

• Restricting components using a registry

• Preventing arbitrary code execution

• Protecting against XSS attacks

• Implementing proper permission checks

• Keeping business logic on the backend

• Building a defense-in-depth architecture for enterprise React applications
By the end of this article, you’ll have a clear understanding of how to make your Configurable UI not only flexible and scalable but also secure enough for production environments.
I also recommend adding a small note like this immediately after the introduction:
📖 Missed the previous article?
Before diving into security, I recommend reading “Building Configurable UI in React:Stop Changing Code for Every UI Update- AI”, where I cover the architecture, component registry, renderer engine, and configuration-driven approach that this article builds upon.
Why Security Becomes More Important
Let’s start with a simple example.
Imagine your backend sends this configuration:
{
"component": "DeleteUserButton"
}If your frontend blindly renders whatever it receives, you’ve already introduced a security risk.
Your application is now trusting external data to decide which components should appear.
Even if your backend is secure today, bugs happen. APIs evolve. Configurations change. Someone might accidentally expose an internal component that was never meant for regular users.
A configurable system should never assume that every configuration is safe.
The first rule is simple:
Treat configuration exactly like user input.
Only Render Components You Know About
One mistake I often see is rendering components dynamically without any validation.
const Component = registry[config.component];
return <Component />;Although this looks clean, it’s incomplete.
Instead, maintain a registry of approved components.
const registry = {
text: TextField,
button: Button,
dropdown: Dropdown
};Now before rendering, verify that the component actually exists.
const Component = registry[config.component];
if (!Component) {
return null;
}
return <Component {...config} />;If the component isn’t registered, ignore it.
Your application should only render components that you explicitly allow.
Don’t Turn JSON into JavaScript
Sometimes teams try to make configurations “smarter” by allowing JavaScript inside them.
For example:
{
"onClick": "deleteAllUsers()"
}or
{
"condition": "user.role === 'ADMIN'"
}This might feel flexible, but it’s also one of the fastest ways to introduce security problems.
Avoid using:
eval()
new Function()Instead, let your configuration reference predefined actions.
const actions = {
save,
cancel,
download
};
actions[config.action]?.();Your configuration should describe what should happen, not contain executable code.
Validate Every Configuration
Even trusted APIs can return incorrect data.
Maybe a required property is missing.
Maybe a field type is misspelled.
Maybe an older backend sends a different structure.
Instead of discovering these problems during rendering, validate the configuration first.
Libraries like Zod, Yup, or JSON Schema make this easy.
If the configuration doesn’t match the expected schema, stop rendering and display an appropriate error.
Failing early is always better than rendering unpredictable UI.
Visibility Isn’t Authorization
This is one of the most common mistakes in frontend applications.
Suppose your configuration contains:
{
"visible": true
}Many developers immediately render the component.
But visibility alone doesn’t mean the user is allowed to perform the action.
Instead, combine configuration with user permissions.
if (
config.visible &&
permissions.includes("DELETE_USER")
) {
return <DeleteButton />;
}The configuration controls the layout.
Permissions control access.
These are two completely different responsibilities.
The Backend Is Still the Source of Truth
Hiding a button doesn’t make your application secure.
Even if the Delete button isn’t visible, anyone can still open Postman, inspect network requests, or call your APIs directly.
That’s why the backend must always validate:

• Authentication

• Authorization

• User roles

• Business rules

• Resource ownership
Think of frontend permissions as improving the user experience.
Think of backend permissions as protecting your system.
Be Careful with HTML
Many configurable systems allow administrators to write formatted content.
For example:
{
"description": "<script>alert('Hello')</script>"
}Rendering this directly creates an XSS vulnerability.
Whenever possible, render plain text.
If HTML is required, sanitize it before rendering using a trusted library such as DOMPurify.
Never assume HTML coming from an API is safe.
Avoid Dynamic API Endpoints
Another pattern I’ve seen is allowing configurations like this:
{
"endpoint": "https://example.com/api"
}This gives configuration too much control.
Instead, let the configuration reference an identifier.
{
"endpoint": "USER_LIST"
}Then map it inside your application.
const endpoints = {
USER_LIST: "/api/users",
PRODUCTS: "/api/products"
};This gives your application complete control over which APIs can be called.
Log Unexpected Configurations
Imagine your frontend suddenly receives:
{
"component": "DeleteDatabase"
}Instead of crashing — or trying to render it — your application should:

• Ignore it

• Log the event

• Send telemetry

• Alert monitoring tools
These logs become incredibly valuable when debugging production issues or identifying suspicious activity.
Keep Business Logic Out of Configuration
Configuration should describe the interface.
It shouldn’t decide whether users are allowed to perform business actions.
Instead of sending:
{
"canApprove": true
}Let the backend decide whether approval is actually allowed.
The frontend can display the button.
The backend performs the real authorization when the user clicks it.
That’s the difference between a secure application and one that simply looks secure.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A Simple Security Checklist
Whenever I’m working on a Configurable UI, I usually go through this checklist:

• Validate every configuration

• Render only registered components

• Never execute JavaScript from configuration

• Keep business logic on the backend

• Validate permissions separately

• Sanitize HTML content

• Restrict API endpoints

• Log unknown configurations

• Version your configuration schema

• Treat configuration as untrusted input
These small practices significantly reduce the chances of introducing security vulnerabilities.Final Thoughts
Configurable UI is one of the most powerful architectural patterns you can adopt in React.
It makes applications flexible, reusable, and much easier to maintain.
But with flexibility comes responsibility.
The frontend should never become a system that blindly executes whatever the backend sends. Instead, it should act as a safe rendering engine — one that validates configuration, enforces boundaries, and only renders trusted components.
One idea has stayed with me throughout every configurable system I’ve worked on:
Your configuration should describe the UI — not control security, execute code, or make business decisions.
If you build your application with that mindset, you’ll end up with a Configurable UI that is not only dynamic and scalable, but also secure enough for enterprise applications.

Thanks for reading! 👋
If you’re interested in React architecture, scalable frontend systems, Configurable UI, performance optimization, and enterprise engineering, follow ThePlainScript. I regularly share practical lessons, real-world patterns, and implementation strategies that you can apply directly to your projects.
